FlatLine / PyEncoder Lifecycle Phase Architecture
==================================================
Whitepaper for GPT Code Models — Implementation Guide
Date: 2026-05-02

This document explains the lifecycle phase architecture used in PyEncoder (and TrioDesktop/FlatLine
projects). It is written for GPT code models who will be implementing or auditing code. Read it
completely before touching anything related to process startup, subprocess calls, threading, or
long-running work.


CORE RULE
---------

If it can hang, crash, block, install, probe tools, make network calls, run long, or own app work,
it must be a lifecycle Phase with a TTL, a PID, and error/fault/timeout callbacks.

No exceptions. The debugger must survive everything.


WHY THIS ARCHITECTURE EXISTS
-----------------------------

The FlatLine debugger is the parent process. It must remain alive when the app child crashes,
a dependency install hangs, a font install triggers UAC, a tool probe takes 60 seconds, or a
network call never returns.

If the debugger and the blocking work share the same OS process and the same Python interpreter,
then a deadlock in the blocking work kills the debugger too. That defeats the entire purpose.

The architecture enforces a hard boundary:

    start.py (parent debugger / supervisor)
        ├── Owns lifecycle phases
        ├── Key loop stays alive (D → debugger console)
        ├── Records phase/process status to DB
        ├── TTL enforcement + taskkill on timeout
        └── Launches workers as child OS processes

    gtp.py / main app (child process)
        ├── Dies without killing parent
        ├── Reports crash info back
        └── Can be relaunched

The parent debugger MUST be able to:
  - Print status while workers run
  - Kill a timed-out worker with taskkill /T /F
  - Relaunch after a worker fault
  - Accept D key input at any point during startup


THE TWO PHASE CLASSES
----------------------

There are two Phase classes. Know both.

1. start.py Phase — used by the parent launcher lifecycle controller (StartLifecycleController)

    Phase(
        phaseId=100,
        key='my-work-key',
        name='My Work',
        process=StartProcess(...),   # or callback=...
        ttl=120.0,                   # REQUIRED — seconds before kill
        group='startup',
        onComplete=lambda p: ...,
        onError=lambda p: ...,
        onFault=lambda p: ...,
        onException=lambda p: ...,
        onTimeout=lambda p: ...,
    )

2. classes/AppLifeCycle.py LifeCyclePhase — used by the app child lifecycle controller

    LifeCyclePhase(
        key='encode-audio',
        callback=self._encodeAudio,
        label='Encode Audio',
        on_error=self._onEncodeError,
        on_fault=self._onEncodeFault,
        on_exception=self._onEncodeException,
        timeout_seconds=120.0,       # REQUIRED
        ttl_seconds=180.0,           # REQUIRED
        process=my_process,
    )

Both require TTL. No TTL = the phase can hang forever = the debugger can hang forever.


THE StartProcess CLASS
-----------------------

Every OS child process must be wrapped in StartProcess. Never call subprocess.Popen directly
except inside an approved lifecycle wrapper function.

    # WRONG — raw Popen blocks the debugger on hang
    proc = subprocess.Popen(['ffmpeg', '-i', input_path, output_path])
    proc.wait()   # hangs forever if ffmpeg hangs

    # CORRECT
    process = StartProcess(
        command=['ffmpeg', '-i', input_path, output_path],
        name='FFmpegEncode',
        ttlSeconds=300.0,
        cwd=str(BASE_DIR),
    )
    process.onError(lambda p: print(f'[WARN:encode] ffmpeg error exit={p.returnCode}'))
    process.onTimeout(lambda p: print(f'[WARN:encode] ffmpeg TTL expired'))
    process.onFault(lambda p: print(f'[WARN:encode] ffmpeg fault: {p.error}'))
    process.start()
    pid = process.pid   # ALWAYS capture the pid

StartProcess.start() automatically:
  - Registers with the lifecycle controller
  - Creates a DB process record with pid, command, phase key
  - Sets up TTL enforcement via superviseProcesses()
  - Kills with taskkill /T /F on TTL expiry
  - Fires onTimeout, onFault, onError callbacks


PID IS MANDATORY
----------------

Every StartProcess.start() call must be followed by pid capture. The lifecycle controller uses
the pid to:
  - Write to the process DB table
  - taskkill on TTL expiry
  - Report to the debugger surface

    process.start()
    pid = process.pid      # REQUIRED on the next line or within 5 lines

If the process object is returned or stored, the pid must be accessible via process.pid at
the call site. The lifecycle bypass detector (lifecyclebypassdetector.py) will flag .start()
calls with no .pid reference in the next 5 lines.

    # OK — pid accessed via return value
    proc = process.start()
    stored_pid = proc.pid

    # OK — pid on process object accessed later
    self.encodeProcess = process
    process.start()
    print(f'encoder pid={self.encodeProcess.pid}')

    # FLAGGED — no pid capture
    process.start()
    doNextThing()   # detector flags this


TTL IS MANDATORY
----------------

Every Phase and StartProcess must have a TTL. The TTL is the wall-clock seconds after which
the lifecycle controller kills the process and fires onTimeout.

Rule of thumb for TTL values:

    Font install worker:       300s  (UAC + copy + registry)
    Dependency pip install:    600s  (slow network)
    Model catalog probe:        60s  (network HTTP)
    FFmpeg encode:             None? Use per-file estimate + 60s buffer
    Database migration:         30s
    File hash check:            10s
    Tool probe (where/which):    5s
    Headless smoke test:        30s

If you cannot estimate a TTL, use 300s and add a comment explaining why.

    # WRONG
    Phase(phaseId=10, key='my-phase', callback=myCallback)

    # WRONG — ttl=0 is treated as no TTL
    Phase(phaseId=10, key='my-phase', callback=myCallback, ttl=0)

    # CORRECT
    Phase(phaseId=10, key='my-phase', callback=myCallback, ttl=120.0)


ERROR / FAULT / TIMEOUT CALLBACKS
----------------------------------

Every process phase should register all three signal handlers. The minimum set is
onError + onTimeout. onFault and onException add extra safety.

Definitions:
  onError    — process exited with non-zero return code
  onFault    — something structurally wrong (e.g. process never started, command not found)
  onException — Python exception during phase execution
  onTimeout  — TTL expired; process was killed

Example with all handlers:

    def _onEncodeError(phase_or_process, payload=None):
        rc = getattr(phase_or_process, 'returnCode', None) or getattr(phase_or_process, 'error', None)
        print(f'[WARN:encode] process error exit={rc}', file=sys.stderr, flush=True)
        recordException('encoder.error', None)

    def _onEncodeFault(phase_or_process, payload=None):
        reason = getattr(phase_or_process, 'faultReason', '') or getattr(phase_or_process, 'error', '')
        print(f'[WARN:encode] process fault: {reason}', file=sys.stderr, flush=True)

    def _onEncodeTimeout(phase_or_process, payload=None):
        print(f'[WARN:encode] TTL expired — killing encoder', file=sys.stderr, flush=True)

    def _onEncodeException(phase_or_process, payload=None):
        tb = getattr(phase_or_process, 'tracebackText', '') or ''
        print(f'[WARN:encode] exception:\n{tb}', file=sys.stderr, flush=True)

    process = StartProcess(
        command=build_ffmpeg_command(job),
        name=f'FFmpegEncode:{job.output_name}',
        ttlSeconds=estimate_encode_ttl(job),
    )
    process.onError(_onEncodeError)
    process.onFault(_onEncodeFault)
    process.onTimeout(_onEncodeTimeout)
    process.onException(_onEncodeException)
    process.start()
    pid = process.pid
    print(f'[TRACE:encode] started pid={pid} job={job.output_name}', file=sys.stderr, flush=True)


WAIT/JOIN ALWAYS NEEDS A TIMEOUT
----------------------------------

Never call .wait(), .join(), or .communicate() without a timeout. Without a timeout they
block forever if the child hangs.

    # WRONG
    process.wait()
    thread.join()
    stdout, stderr = proc.communicate()

    # CORRECT
    try:
        process.wait(timeout=30)
    except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
        process.kill()
        print('[WARN:encode] wait timed out — killed', file=sys.stderr, flush=True)

    thread.join(timeout=10)
    if thread.is_alive():
        print('[WARN] thread still alive after join', file=sys.stderr, flush=True)

    try:
        stdout, stderr = proc.communicate(timeout=60)
    except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
        proc.kill()
        stdout, stderr = proc.communicate()

The lifecycle bypass detector flags all .wait()/.join()/.communicate() calls that have
no timeout argument (positional or keyword).


NO THREADS IN THE PARENT DEBUGGER
-----------------------------------

The parent start.py debugger must have zero threading.Thread() calls (other than the single
approved heartbeat worker in debugger_surface.py which has # thread-ok).

Every unit of blocking work that previously used a thread must be converted to:

    1. A StartProcess — separate OS process launched via python start.py --worker-flag
    2. Or an asyncio task — only for I/O-bound non-blocking work

Fake process wrappers are worse than threads because they lie to the detector:

    # LYING — this is a thread dressed up as a process
    class ProcessJob:
        def start(self):
            threading.Thread(target=self.run).start()   # WRONG

    # CORRECT — real OS process boundary
    class FontInstallWorker:
        def start(self, argv):
            return StartProcess(
                command=[sys.executable, str(Path(__file__).resolve()), '--font-worker', *argv],
                name='TerminalFontWorker',
                ttlSeconds=300.0,
            ).start()


WORKER PATTERN — HOW TO SPLIT BLOCKING WORK
---------------------------------------------

The canonical pattern for any blocking work:

    In start.py:

        # 1. Worker entry point
        def runMyWorker(argv=None) -> int:
            print(f'[TRACE:my-worker] started pid={os.getpid()}', file=sys.stderr, flush=True)
            try:
                do_the_blocking_work()
                print('[TRACE:my-worker] complete', file=sys.stderr, flush=True)
                return 0
            except BaseException as error:
                print(f'[FATAL:my-worker] {traceback.format_exc()}', file=sys.stderr, flush=True)
                return 1

        # 2. Worker request detection
        MY_WORKER_FLAGS = buildCliAliasSet('my-worker', 'myworker')

        def myWorkerRequested(argv=None) -> bool:
            return bool(
                cliHasAnyFlag(list(argv or []), MY_WORKER_FLAGS)
                or os.environ.get('MY_WORKER', '').strip().lower() in {'1', 'true', 'yes'}
            )

        # 3. Lifecycle phase that launches the worker
        def myWorkerPhase(self):
            env = os.environ.copy()
            env['MY_WORKER'] = '1'
            process = StartProcess(
                command=[sys.executable, str(Path(__file__).resolve()), '--my-worker', *self.argv],
                name='MyWorker',
                ttlSeconds=120.0,
                cwd=str(BASE_DIR),
                env=env,
            )
            process.onTimeout(lambda p: print('[WARN:my-worker] TTL expired', file=sys.stderr, flush=True))
            process.onError(lambda p: print(f'[WARN:my-worker] error exit={p.returnCode}', file=sys.stderr, flush=True))
            self.startExistingProcessPhase(process)
            pid = process.pid
            print(f'[TRACE:my-worker] worker started pid={pid}', file=sys.stderr, flush=True)
            # Optionally wait with timeout
            try:
                process.wait(timeout=110)
            except Exception:
                pass
            return True

        # 4. In the main() short-circuit, route the worker entry
        if myWorkerRequested(argv):
            return int(runMyWorker(argv))

        # 5. In the phase manifest
        PHASE_MANIFEST = tuple(startPhaseSpec(key, method) for key, method in parsePipeSpecRows('''
        ...
        parent-bootstrap-my-work|myWorkerPhase
        ...
        '''))

    In gtp.py / AppLifeCycle controller (for app-side work):

        phase = LifeCyclePhase(
            key='my-work',
            callback=self._doMyWork,
            label='My Work',
            on_error=self._onMyWorkError,
            on_fault=self._onMyWorkFault,
            on_exception=self._onMyWorkException,
            timeout_seconds=120.0,
            ttl_seconds=120.0,
        )
        self.lifecycle.register_phase(phase)


ELEVATION (UAC) RULE
---------------------

UAC prompts must ONLY happen inside a dedicated worker subprocess launched by the lifecycle.
Never in the parent launcher, never in bootstrapRuntimePathsPhase, never inline in main().

    # WRONG — blocks the parent on an invisible UAC dialog
    def maybeRecommendConsoleFont(argv):
        for spec in _fontInstallAttemptSpecs(font_path):
            spec['runner']()   # calls _attemptWindowsElevatedFontInstallAndSwitch inline

    # CORRECT — elevation only inside the font worker process
    def _fontInstallAttemptSpecs(font_path):
        in_font_worker = os.environ.get('FONT_WORKER', '').strip().lower() in {'1','true'}
        specs = []
        if in_font_worker:
            specs.append({'key': 'elevated', 'runner': lambda: _attemptElevatedInstall(font_path)})
        specs.append({'key': 'user', 'runner': lambda: _installFontForCurrentUser(font_path)})
        return specs

The pattern: set an environment variable before launching the worker subprocess. Check that
variable inside the function that does the dangerous work. This ensures the dangerous code
only runs when the parent has already isolated it in a child process.


BLOCKING NETWORK CALLS
-----------------------

Any function that makes HTTP requests (requests.get, httpx.get, openai client calls, etc.)
must run inside a worker process or have an explicit timeout.

    # WRONG — hangs if server is down
    response = requests.get('http://localhost:11434/api/tags')

    # CORRECT
    response = requests.get('http://localhost:11434/api/tags', timeout=10)

    # BEST — in a worker process with TTL
    # parent: launch modelCatalogWorkerPhase (ttl=60)
    # worker: catalog.loadLatestDeployableVersions(allowNetwork=True)
    #         uses requests with timeout internally


THE DETECTOR — lifecyclebypassdetector.py
-----------------------------------------

The lifecycle bypass detector scans all .py files under the project root (except vendor/,
build/, dist/, logs/) and enforces five rules:

Rule 1: DIRECT_SPAWN
    Raw subprocess/threading/os.system calls outside approved wrapper functions.
    Flagged when the call is not inside one of:
        launcherRunCommand, launcherStartProcess, _early_run_command,
        managedSubprocessRun, managedSubprocessPopen, StartProcess (class),
        AppLifeCycle (class), taskkill_process, etc.

Rule 2: NO_TTL
    Phase(), StartProcess(), LifeCyclePhase() constructed without any of:
        ttl=, ttlSeconds=, ttl_seconds=, timeout=, timeout_seconds=, timeoutSeconds=

Rule 3: NO_PID
    .start() called on a process-like variable (process, proc, worker, p, child, sp...)
    with no .pid reference in the next 5 lines.

Rule 4: BARE_SLEEP
    time.sleep() at module scope (top-level code that blocks the interpreter on import).

Rule 5: BLOCKING_NO_TIMEOUT
    .wait(), .join(), or .communicate() called without a timeout argument.

Run the detector:

    python start.py --lifecycle-bypass
    # or directly:
    python vendor/claude/lifecyclebypassdetector.py --root . --output logs/lifecyclebypass.txt .

Suppression markers (inline comment on or within 2 lines of the flagged line):
    # lifecycle-bypass-ok   — approved raw spawn, document why
    # ttl-ok                — ttl set elsewhere or not applicable
    # pid-ok                — pid captured elsewhere
    # sleep-ok              — intentional sleep
    # block-ok              — blocking call with intentional no-timeout
    # phase-ownership-ok    — pre-existing approved call site


HOW TO FIX EACH FINDING TYPE
------------------------------

DIRECT_SPAWN findings:

    CASE: build script / standalone tool (CPython-Build-and-Run-Script.py, rebuild_*.py)
        These are not lifecycle-managed scripts. They run standalone.
        Add # lifecycle-bypass-ok: standalone build script on the call site.

    CASE: inside a class that IS a lifecycle wrapper (AppLifeCycle, StartProcess)
        Add # lifecycle-ok to the line. The detector allowlists these classes but the
        marker prevents future confusion.

    CASE: inside an app class that is NOT a lifecycle wrapper
        Convert to StartProcess. Example:

            # BEFORE (classes/EncoderEngine.py)
            self.process = subprocess.Popen(self.command, cwd=self.cwd, env=self.env)

            # AFTER
            self._startProcess = StartProcess(
                command=self.command,
                name=f'EncoderProcess:{self.job_id}',
                ttlSeconds=self.estimated_ttl,
                cwd=self.cwd,
                env=self.env,
            )
            self._startProcess.onError(self._onProcessError)
            self._startProcess.onTimeout(self._onProcessTimeout)
            self._startProcess.start()
            self.pid = self._startProcess.pid

    CASE: QThread in a Qt widget
        QThread inside a widget is acceptable if it's a short-lived I/O helper.
        Add # lifecycle-bypass-ok: Qt UI thread — short-lived, no blocking install/probe
        If it's long-running or does blocking work, move it to a StartProcess worker.

NO_TTL findings:

    Always add the ttl parameter. Use the table above for guidance.

    If the Phase/Process is genuinely instantaneous (e.g. reads a config file),
    use ttl=10.0 and add a comment.

    If the ttl is set on a parent object and passed down:
        Add # ttl-ok: ttl set by caller to suppress the finding.

NO_PID findings:

    Add pid capture after every .start() call.

    If the process object is stored on self and pid is read later:
        process.start()
        self.process = process
        # pid-ok: pid available via self.process.pid

    If the return value of start() IS the process:
        proc = process.start()
        pid = proc.pid

BLOCKING_NO_TIMEOUT findings:

    Always add a timeout. Every .wait(), .join(), .communicate() needs one.

    For .wait() inside a lifecycle phase:
        try:
            process.wait(timeout=max(5, ttl - 10))
        except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
            process.kill()

    For threading.Event.wait() (like consoleWake.wait()):
        Add a polling timeout: self.consoleWake.wait(timeout=1.0)
        The event loop should re-check stop conditions at each timeout.

    For .join() on a worker thread at shutdown:
        thread.join(timeout=3.0)
        if thread.is_alive():
            print(f'[WARN:shutdown] {thread.name} still alive', file=sys.stderr, flush=True)


COMPLETE WORKED EXAMPLE — Export Worker
-----------------------------------------

This shows the full pattern: phase registration, process launch, pid capture,
TTL, error/timeout handling, and DB persistence.

    # In classes/AppLifeCycle.py or a lifecycle controller:

    class ExportLifecycleController:

        def register_export_phases(self, job: ExportJob) -> None:
            self.lifecycle.register_phase(LifeCyclePhase(
                key=f'export-encode:{job.id}',
                callback=lambda: self._launch_export_worker(job),
                label=f'Export Encode: {job.output_name}',
                on_error=lambda p: self._on_export_error(p, job),
                on_fault=lambda p: self._on_export_fault(p, job),
                on_exception=lambda p: self._on_export_exception(p, job),
                on_complete=lambda p: self._on_export_complete(p, job),
                timeout_seconds=self._estimate_encode_ttl(job),
                ttl_seconds=self._estimate_encode_ttl(job) + 30,
            ))

        def _launch_export_worker(self, job: ExportJob) -> bool:
            env = os.environ.copy()
            env['EXPORT_WORKER'] = '1'
            env['EXPORT_JOB_ID'] = str(job.id)
            process = StartProcess(
                command=[
                    sys.executable,
                    str(Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / 'start.py'),
                    '--export-worker',
                    '--job-id', str(job.id),
                ],
                name=f'ExportWorker:{job.id}',
                ttlSeconds=self._estimate_encode_ttl(job) + 30,
                cwd=str(_repo_root()),
                env=env,
            )
            process.onError(lambda p: self._on_export_process_error(p, job))
            process.onTimeout(lambda p: self._on_export_timeout(p, job))
            process.onFault(lambda p: self._on_export_fault(p, job))
            process.start()
            pid = process.pid
            print(
                f'[TRACE:export] worker started pid={pid} job={job.id} output={job.output_name}',
                file=sys.stderr, flush=True,
            )
            try:
                process.wait(timeout=self._estimate_encode_ttl(job))
            except Exception:
                pass
            return True

        def _on_export_timeout(self, process, job: ExportJob) -> None:
            pid = getattr(process, 'pid', 0) or 0
            print(
                f'[WARN:export] TTL expired pid={pid} job={job.id} — killing',
                file=sys.stderr, flush=True,
            )
            job.status = 'timeout'
            job.error = f'TTL expired after {process.ttlSeconds}s'

        def _on_export_error(self, process, job: ExportJob) -> None:
            rc = getattr(process, 'returnCode', None)
            print(
                f'[WARN:export] error exit={rc} job={job.id}',
                file=sys.stderr, flush=True,
            )
            job.status = 'error'

        def _estimate_encode_ttl(self, job: ExportJob) -> float:
            # Rough estimate: 2 seconds per second of timeline + 60s buffer
            duration = float(getattr(job, 'duration_seconds', 0.0) or 0.0)
            return max(60.0, duration * 2 + 60)


COMPLETE WORKED EXAMPLE — Headless Mode
-----------------------------------------

Per the whitepaper from the previous bot:

    # WRONG — headless is a fake half-app
    def headlessMain():
        doTinySqlTest()
        constructHalfApp()
        return 0

    # CORRECT — headless runs the real lifecycle, skips Qt phases
    def main():
        lifecycle = AppLifeCycleController(headless=args.headless)
        lifecycle.register_phases([
            LifeCyclePhase(
                key='db-connect',
                callback=self._connectDb,
                timeout_seconds=10.0,
                ttl_seconds=10.0,
            ),
            LifeCyclePhase(
                key='qt-init',
                callback=self._initQt,
                timeout_seconds=30.0,
                ttl_seconds=30.0,
                metadata={'includesQT': True, 'canRunHeadless': False},
            ),
            LifeCyclePhase(
                key='show-window',
                callback=self._showMainWindow,
                timeout_seconds=15.0,
                ttl_seconds=15.0,
                metadata={'includesQT': True, 'canRunHeadless': False},
            ),
        ])
        return lifecycle.run()

    # Inside AppLifeCycleController.run():
    for phase in self.phases:
        if self.headless and not phase.metadata.get('canRunHeadless', True):
            print(f'[HEADLESS] skipping {phase.key} includesQT={phase.metadata.get("includesQT")}')
            continue
        self._run_phase(phase)


READING THE DETECTOR REPORT
-----------------------------

The detector writes logs/lifecyclebypass.txt. Run it with:

    python start.py --lifecycle-bypass

The report groups findings by rule:

    DIRECT_SPAWN — raw spawns that need lifecycle wrapping or suppression markers
    NO_TTL       — constructors missing ttl
    NO_PID       — .start() with no pid capture
    BLOCKING_NO_TIMEOUT — .wait()/.join()/.communicate() without timeout

For each finding:

    file.py:lineno: RULE_NAME ClassName.method_name: source line

Priority order:
    1. Fix NO_TTL and NO_PID first — these are silent hang risks
    2. Fix DIRECT_SPAWN for app code (ignore build scripts with the marker)
    3. Fix BLOCKING_NO_TIMEOUT for anything in the parent debugger path
    4. BLOCKING_NO_TIMEOUT in build scripts is lower priority


CHECKLIST BEFORE HANDOFF
--------------------------

Before declaring work complete on any session that touches process/thread/phase code:

[ ] python start.py --lifecycle-bypass shows 0 NEW findings (existing marked ones OK)
[ ] python start.py --threads shows 0 Thread/Process findings
[ ] Every StartProcess() has ttlSeconds= set
[ ] Every StartProcess().start() is followed by pid = process.pid within 5 lines
[ ] Every .wait()/.join()/.communicate() has a timeout= argument
[ ] No threading.Thread() in start.py outside debugger_surface.py
[ ] No elevation (UAC/RunAs) outside the font worker subprocess
[ ] No time.sleep() at module scope
[ ] No blocking HTTP calls without timeout=

End of whitepaper.
